author: @martinesimmonds5590
text:
Hi Robert.
I'm wondering if you've ever made a generator that uses both polarities of a magnet? It has always been a gripe of mine, why modern motors and generators use only half a magnets potential. My own experiments show double the amperage, for very little extra cost. Yes, I know a co-axial permanent magnet generator is a pain to make, but nessaccery to take advantage of both polarities. Doubling the amperage for a few pounds or pennies more seems worthy and can be scaled to any size. You can play around with how you wire up the coils and be bemused, or try to understand why the dual polarity Motor/Generator is not in common use.

4:56 to avoid having to stop and lever the wooden plug out of the hole saw, insert a small gerbil into the holesaw before you start. Play Queen's We Will Rock You when you start drilling and the gerbil will stamp the plug out saving you lots of time on a long production run


HTH

author: @boblewis5558
text:
The music is cheery enough but unfortunately it's the same bloody royalty free tune that EVERYBODY uses! LOL It's about time YouTube produced a LARGE royalty free music library for video makers to use! OR that music publishers relaxed their stance on re-use for the hobbyist market. It's not like it's the music that attracts people to watch a "maker's" video after all, such as big advertisers use for example! Everyone remembers the Peugot advert for "Take My Breath Away" and not the specific model of the car, even if they remember the MAKE of the car - A BENEFIT to the artist for which they were paid a handsome royalty no doubt from the licence fee. That isn't the case with the vast majority of YouTube video makers.

Having no music is better than none in my view. Having easily readable subtitles would be preferable. In addition, as an ex studio sound engineer, I can tell you the sound quality is of vastly greater importance. Having a clip mic recording the sound to the camera rather than relying on the camera to pick up decent quality from 6 to 10 feet away will make a HUGE noticeable improvement. Connecting that mic to a digital voice recorder in your pocket (even to another phone!) will enable you to be heard far more clearly close up and equally well when moving about or further from camera. A simple, old fashioned, in-shot, clapperboard or hand clap can be used in post production to sync the entire digital sound track in one go AND provides it as a completely separate recording stream to make further editing a breeze (if needed). No need for poor quality or unbalanced audio any more Robert! ;-)

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Not a critique, I enjoyed the project.  Just for your next scrapyard visit -- the automatic opening doors on hatchbacks and minivans also use a very sturdy version of the toothed belt, as well as motors and gears made to engage them.

author: @krap101
text: Is there a reason why you needed gear teeth opposed to something like a pulley and belt setup?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i wanted to show how to make the gears - like i said in the video - i couldn't have shown that if i had made a pully

replies.author: @krap101
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering That makes sense. I suppose what I meant to ask is what the advantage was. It was an intriguing idea given that gears can carry more torque than belts without slipping, but you see v belts and such driving heavy machinery such as lathes. For this specific application it seemed like you might not see the benefit.
